comparisonBoundary: Earlier event wikis are comparison context only. They are not primary evidence for World's Fair 2026 claims, and no local fixture is represented as a public live site.

stages.label: World's Fair 2026
stages.evidenceRole: current event synthesis backed by linked local evidence

evolution.wf2024: Coding agents sat inside a broader code-generation and AI-engineering discipline.
evolution.miami2026: The focus narrowed to developer workflow, agent-ready interfaces, runtimes, IDEs, context, and quality gates.
evolution.wf2025: The comparison corpus emphasized shipping, evaluating, securing, and reviewing production coding agents.
evolution.wf2026: The linked conference graph frames coding agents as software-factory components governed by eval gates, context systems, and execution boundaries.

themes.whyNow: The unit of engineering is shifting from an isolated assistant interaction to a repeatable production system that delegates, verifies, and records software work.
themes.practicalLesson: Evaluate the complete agent loop: task definition, context assembly, tool permissions, sandbox execution, verification, review, and provenance. Model capability alone is not the system boundary.

evolution.wf2024: Evals were one foundational part of the emerging AI-engineering discipline.
evolution.miami2026: Quality gates became part of day-to-day developer-agent workflow and auditability.
evolution.wf2025: The comparison corpus treated evals, observability, and human-seeded checks as production requirements.
evolution.wf2026: The linked synthesis layer treats evaluation as an operational gate connecting agent work, review, provenance, and release decisions.

themes.whyNow: Autonomous and long-running agent work increases the cost of discovering errors only after output reaches users or a codebase.
themes.practicalLesson: Define acceptance evidence before delegation, retain the run artifacts needed to explain a result, and make failed checks stop or route the workflow instead of merely producing a dashboard score.

evolution.wf2024: RAG and context systems were broad architectural categories.
evolution.miami2026: Context engineering and agent auditability moved into the developer workflow.
evolution.wf2025: The comparison corpus separated agent memory from generic RAG and connected it to production behavior.
evolution.wf2026: The linked evidence graph frames memory as governed context infrastructure with provenance, retrieval, and evaluation requirements.

themes.whyNow: Longer-running agents need continuity across tasks, but unbounded memory can amplify stale facts, hidden assumptions, and sensitive data.
themes.practicalLesson: Store provenance and lifecycle metadata with remembered context, make retrieval inspectable, and evaluate whether memory improves the task instead of measuring recall volume alone.

evolution.wf2024: Human oversight and infrastructure were broad safeguards around AI systems.
evolution.miami2026: Remote agents and sandboxed compute made runtime boundaries part of developer workflow.
evolution.wf2025: The comparison corpus foregrounded containment, identity, OAuth, access control, and code-execution safety.
evolution.wf2026: The linked conference graph treats sandboxes, tool permissions, provenance, and eval gates as one operational control surface.

themes.whyNow: Agents increasingly execute code and use credentials rather than only returning text, so correctness and blast-radius control must be designed together.
themes.practicalLesson: Give each run the minimum tools, credentials, network reach, filesystem scope, and lifetime it needs; capture actions and outputs so approval and incident review use the same evidence trail.

evolution.wf2024: Tool and model interfaces were part of a broad framework and infrastructure landscape.
evolution.miami2026: MCP versus skills became a concrete developer-workflow and interface-design decision.
evolution.wf2025: The comparison corpus emphasized enterprise readiness, observability, protection, and real-world MCP integration.
evolution.wf2026: The linked conference graph treats MCP as a governed tool boundary involving discovery, UI, permissions, runtime behavior, and auditability.

themes.whyNow: A standard interface expands interoperability, but it also concentrates trust decisions around tool discovery, authorization, execution, and returned context.
themes.practicalLesson: Evaluate an MCP integration as a capability boundary: enumerate exposed actions, authorize them narrowly, test host behavior, log invocations, and verify how returned data enters model context.
